text: This article describes a bit the history of semantic web Efforts and it leads to a couple of interesting threads that I wanted to share with you

elements.text: This article describes a bit the history of semantic web Efforts and it leads to a couple of interesting threads that I wanted to share with you

text: Here is the first interesting aspect: <https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=25907346> I came to the link through this hackernews entry and its interesting to read the comments. Uncovering quite a separation between when is described as "academic" semantic web efforts and some examples from real (engineering?)  implementations. Quite the revelation on how the institution of science is failing but also how discussion culture in society is in seriously  desolate state. (IMHO)

elements.text: Here is the first interesting aspect:

text:
I shared then this thread with the hyperlink academy <https://forum.hyperlink.academy/t/roamlab-a-framework-for-building-community-labs/1811/5?u=curious_reader>  I will try to summarize my view from there here again. Referring to the semantic web article it seems interesting to me that after all these developments in the semantic web realm there are now clusters emerging around what is called personal knowledge graphs. Even if the article only mentions knowledge graphs I do find it very important to put emphasis on the _personal_ aspect of knowledge graphs.
To get people to actually care about software and knowledge structures it seems like a good idea to stark with the organisation of _personal_ knowledge  because they can much easier relate to that. Which in turn has lead to bloom in tools to assist that purpose: roam , zettls, athens etc etc..

attachments.title: RoamLab: A Framework for Building Community Labs
attachments.text: hi @adolforismos! A couple of interesting thoughts here. I’m not sure I can respond to all in a coherent manner so I will pick individual statements and try to respond to them If Roam can be used as a learning platform, it can be used just for a specific persona. A writing-driven persona. Some time ago I had a chat with one of the roam creators and some other guys on twitter. The creator mentioned that there was one software project which was more ambitions and that was <https://gtoolkit.c>...
attachments.fallback: Hyperlink Forum: RoamLab: A Framework for Building Community Labs

text: So I see a kind of renaissance as tools like Gtoolkit and potentially unison later aspire to vision of _personal_ computing that was unfulfilled  even though Alan Kay, ted nelson and many others have been dreaming about it since quite some time.

text: Im really looking forward to not only meshed knowledge graphs but also _personal object repositories_ which would enable at least some part of the vision alan kay outlined here <@UAJKEBGP8>: <https://futureofcoding.slack.com/archives/C5U3SEW6A/p1612118402036300>

text:
I joined, but I don't see anything about FoC once I'm in. I just see an empty library, with a "who to follow" sidebar.

You might want to add some content to this group so that we know what we're looking at when we first join. Right now it's hard to tell what the _bones_ of this service are, so that we can hang our mind-meats on them.

text: It’s a very popular “classic” textbook on database theory. It’s got some useful information for the uninitiated, but IMO it’s quite dated now and missing all the newest ideas. Unfortunately, I don’t have any newer recommendations, except “Datalog and Logic Databases” for those interested in Datalog.

elements.text: It’s a very popular “classic” textbook on database theory. It’s got some useful information for the uninitiated, but IMO it’s quite dated now and missing all the newest ideas. Unfortunately, I don’t have any newer recommendations, except “Datalog and Logic Databases” for those interested in Datalog.

text: Love it. I'm always struggling with different ways to surface information from complex graphs, and there are definitely ideas here that I'll want to play with. :slightly_smiling_face:

elements.text: Love it. I'm always struggling with different ways to surface information from complex graphs, and there are definitely ideas here that I'll want to play with.

text:
Yeah, graphs are painful to interact with - which is especially funny considering that most interesting (and thus complex) topics *are* graphs. Indeed, I'm becoming increasingly convinced that all content is a view of some graph, and we're just stuck interacting with whatever projection was chosen (e.g. text).

Anything in particular you liked, were confused about or can think of <@U013ZLJARC7> ?

text: I particularly like the highlighting system :slightly_smiling_face: The only thing that was momentarily confusing was the temporal ordering in the general summary, because "centuries ago" seems like it's in the wrong place in the list.

text:
Ah, you're right, it *is* in the wrong place - should be immediately after "long ago", must have messed up the date range sort.. fixed now.

I'm curious, did you try and edit the query directly? I'm worried it's not obvious that you can do that, but don't want to clutter the interface either.
